skill.name: daily-dev
skill.description: Use daily.dev API guidance to find current developer articles, feeds, tags, bookmarks, and profile data through authenticated daily.dev endpoints.
skill.summary: Find current developer content and daily.dev feed data from authenticated API endpoints.
skill.author: dailydotdev

security_audit.summary: Static findings for command execution, credential handling, filesystem access, and network use are mostly legitimate for an API reference skill, but several are true positives that require disclosure. The skill enables Bash, includes OS credential-store commands, and embeds a curl and jq command that fetches the daily.dev OpenAPI spec; no malicious exfiltration or prompt injection was found.

medium_findings.title: Embedded Bash Network Command in Skill Content
medium_findings.description: The skill contains an embedded command that calls curl and jq to fetch and format the public daily.dev OpenAPI document. This confirms the external command and network findings, although the command targets the documented daily.dev API and does not include the user token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is visible in the skill content and performs a network request through Bash tooling. Confidence is high, but impact is limited because the endpoint is public documentation and no credential is passed.

medium_findings.title: Credential Store and Environment Token Handling
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents or users to retrieve a daily.dev token from macOS Keychain, Windows credential storage, Linux Secret Service, and environment variables. This is expected for an authenticated API skill, but it increases risk because the token becomes available to shell commands and agent context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly retrieve a sensitive token and place it in an environment variable. The behavior is legitimate and scoped to daily.dev, so this is a medium-risk operational concern rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated API Operations Against External Service
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to call daily.dev API endpoints with a bearer token for feeds, bookmarks, profile, tags, and search workflows. This network access is core functionality, but marketplace users should understand that requests leave the local environment and may reflect account-specific preferences.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The authentication header and endpoint workflow descriptions confirm external API use. The destination is the intended daily.dev service, and I found no evidence of requests to unrelated domains.

low_findings.title: Package Installation Commands Are Setup Documentation
low_findings.description: The sudo package manager entries are comments that tell Linux users how to install libsecret tooling. They are not hidden execution logic, but they still require user review before running privileged commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands appear inside a setup code block and are documented package installation examples. No instruction attempts to run them automatically.

low_findings.title: Shell Profile File References Are Persistence Guidance
low_findings.description: References to shell profile files are used to auto-load the daily.dev token from OS credential storage. This is not malicious persistence, but storing token-loading logic in startup files can expand token exposure.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file references are explicit and relate to token loading. The context is user setup documentation, so impact is low if users understand the exposure.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The lines flagged for weak cryptography contain descriptive skill text or API formatting content, not cryptographic algorithms or certificate processing. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, custom crypto, or key misuse in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic operation at the cited lines. The static analyzer appears to have matched text inside documentation or command formatting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tooling Enabled
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and includes shell commands for credential retrieval and API documentation lookup. This is useful for the skill, but it creates execution risk if commands are modified or copied without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and shell snippets are explicit. The intended commands are scoped, but Bash access is still a meaningful marketplace risk factor.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Access Combined With Network API Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill combines token retrieval with authenticated requests to daily.dev. This combination is expected for this API skill, but it must remain limited to api.daily.dev as the skill itself warns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The security warning, token resolution instructions, and base URL confirm the credential plus network pattern. I found no evidence that the token is sent outside the documented daily.dev domain.

content.user_title: Track Current Developer Trends with daily.dev
content.value_statement: Developer news changes faster than model training data. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code query daily.dev feeds, tags, search, bookmarks, and profile data through the daily.dev API.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to authenticate daily.dev API requests with a bearer token.
Shows how to fetch and inspect the public daily.dev OpenAPI specification.
Lists workflows for feeds, tags, search, bookmarks, custom feeds, and profile stack data.
Guides agents to build personalized developer content feeds from project or GitHub context.
Documents daily.dev rate limits and common API error meanings.
limitations: Requires a daily.dev Plus subscription and a valid API token.
Depends on external daily.dev API availability and rate limits.
Does not include a standalone client library or executable application.
May require Bash, curl, jq, or OS credential tools for the documented examples.

use_cases.title: Current Stack Research
use_cases.description: Create a daily.dev feed for the technologies used in a project and surface recent articles, discussions, and tutorials.

use_cases.title: Developer Briefing Automation
use_cases.description: Prepare weekly summaries from followed tags, popular posts, and discussed topics relevant to a team stack.

use_cases.title: Research Workspace Setup
use_cases.description: Build custom feeds and bookmark lists for a deep research topic, then track useful posts over time.

prompt_templates.title: Find Recent Articles
prompt_templates.prompt: Use daily.dev to find recent articles about TypeScript performance. Summarize the most relevant items and include source links.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Project Feed
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this repository stack, map it to daily.dev tags, and suggest a custom feed plan for the main technologies.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Weekly Briefing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use my followed daily.dev topics to prepare a weekly engineering briefing with trends, debates, and practical reading links.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Perspectives
prompt_templates.prompt: Search daily.dev for recent posts about Kubernetes cost optimization, compare source perspectives, and identify where recommendations differ.

output_examples.input: Find what is new in React server components this week.
output_examples.output: A concise briefing with recent daily.dev posts, source links, common themes, and practical notes for an existing React project.

output_examples.input: Set up a learning feed for a Rust command-line project.
output_examples.output: A feed plan that maps repository files to Rust, CLI, testing, and packaging topics, with suggested bookmarks for onboarding.

output_examples.input: What are developers debating about AI code review tools?
output_examples.output: A balanced summary of popular and discussed posts, including agreement points, disagreement points, and links for deeper reading.

best_practices: Keep the daily.dev token in an approved secret store and limit requests to api.daily.dev.
Check rate-limit headers before running repeated feed, search, or bookmark workflows.
Ask for narrow topics, tags, or repository context to improve feed relevance.
anti_patterns: Do not paste a daily.dev API token into chat history or source files.
Do not use broad trend prompts when a project-specific feed would be more useful.
Do not treat popular posts as authoritative without comparing sources and dates.

faq.question: Does this skill require a paid daily.dev account?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documentation says the API requires a daily.dev Plus subscription and a token.

faq.question: Can it access my personalized daily.dev feed?
faq.answer: Yes, if you provide a valid token with access to your daily.dev account data.

faq.question: Does it send my token to third-party domains?
faq.answer: The reviewed skill warns to send the token only to api.daily.dev. No evidence found of other token destinations.

faq.question: Can it create custom feeds and bookmark lists?
faq.answer: The skill documents workflows for custom feeds, bookmarks, followed tags, and research lists through daily.dev endpoints.

faq.question: Does it work without Bash tools?
faq.answer: The API concepts still apply, but the provided examples use Bash, curl, jq, PowerShell, and OS credential tools.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing this skill?
faq.answer: Review Bash access, token storage instructions, and the embedded OpenAPI fetch command before enabling it for users.
